tasks.title: Inventory current completion evidence before adding structured acceptance checks
tasks.reason: Historical learning data still shows a weak feedback loop: codex-learning/metrics.json reports success_rate=0.18 with manual_recovery_records=10, the current board snapshot already contains failed, running, approved, and pending tasks, and earlier registry snapshots showed 11 completed tasks without a compact acceptance-evidence field. A bounded inventory step is still the smallest reversible experiment for tightening learning quality before adding new persistence.
tasks.hypothesis: If the next run documents the exact completion, execution, history, and board-status fields already persisted for successful work and selects one existing write path for a compact acceptance-evidence object, then later evaluator changes can stay deterministic and keep learning outputs aligned with the registry instead of creating another parallel store.
tasks.experiment: Inspect the current completed-task records, the live board snapshot in codex-memory/tasks.json, and agents/evaluator.sh, agents/orchestrator.sh, plus scripts/lib.sh, then write one inventory artifact at codex-memory/completion-evidence-inventory.json describing the exact observed completion fields, the related board-status fields, and the single best insertion point for structured acceptance evidence. Do not change evaluator behavior or task persistence in the same run.
success_criteria: A single JSON artifact at codex-memory/completion-evidence-inventory.json lists the currently observed completion, execution, and history fields used by completed work.
The inventory cites at least one live completed-task example from the historical snapshot rather than inferring schema from memory alone.
The inventory names one existing evaluator or registry write path where compact acceptance evidence can be added without creating a second learning store.
The inventory shows how the chosen evidence fields relate back to the currently persisted board or metrics snapshot without changing either schema in the same run.
The run does not change evaluator behavior, queue semantics, or task lifecycle state beyond creating the inventory artifact and any lifecycle metadata needed to record completion.
tasks.rollback: Delete the inventory artifact and return to the current state where acceptance-evidence work has no dedicated source-of-truth map.

tasks.title: Keep strategy-seeded tasks pending until human approval
tasks.reason: Historical task data still shows task-001, task-002, task-003, task-005, task-006, task-007, task-008, task-010, and task-011 all moved from create to approve immediately with the note that they were auto-approved from strategy settings and queued without human review. Those same nine tasks now account for every failed item on the board, including duplicate clusters like task-001/task-010/task-011 and task-005/task-007 before any human approval checkpoint could merge or reject them. The current snapshot remains stuck at 14 total tasks with 5 pending_approval, 9 failed, and 0 approved while `codex-learning/metrics.json` still reports success_rate=0.17, so the human gate is still not intercepting oversized or duplicate strategy experiments before they consume both retries.
tasks.hypothesis: If strategy-created tasks remain in pending_approval until a human explicitly approves them, duplicated or oversized strategy experiments can be edited, merged, or rejected before queue handoff, which should reduce retry_rate and preserve scarce board capacity for smaller reversible work.
tasks.experiment: Change only the strategy task handoff so newly created strategy tasks stop after the pending_approval write and do not emit an immediate approve transition, queue_handoff, approved_at, or execution_brief until an explicit human approval action occurs. Leave strategy refreshes of existing pending items, manual approval, rejection, queue execution, and non-strategy task handling unchanged.
success_criteria: A newly created strategy task remains in pending_approval with no immediate approve history entry.
No queue_handoff, approved_at, or execution_brief state is written for the same task before a human approval action.
Manual approval through the current approval path still transitions the task to approved and queues it exactly once.
Refreshing an already pending strategy task keeps it in pending_approval and does not enqueue it.
Existing non-strategy submission flows keep their current behavior.
tasks.rollback: Restore the current automatic strategy approval step so strategy-created tasks queue immediately again.

tasks.title: Reject step-text successor tasks before board persistence
tasks.reason: Historical board data still shows task-005 and task-007 were created with the same truncated step-text title copied from a parent dashboard-inspection failure, then auto-approved and driven through two `execute_start` attempts each before failing. Task-007's persisted `failure_context.failed_step` still repeats the same inspection wording, while the current snapshot remains stuck at 14 total tasks with 5 `pending_approval`, 9 `failed`, and 0 `approved`, and `codex-learning/metrics.json` still reports success_rate=0.17. That spillover pattern is still consuming board capacity without producing a smaller executable experiment.
tasks.hypothesis: If successor-task creation compares a candidate title and queue text against the parent task's persisted failed-step text before writing to `codex-memory/tasks.json`, then malformed step-text spillovers will be rejected or forced into a concise rewrite deterministically, which should preserve approval bandwidth for concrete reversible experiments instead of repeating the same failed wording.
tasks.experiment: Change only the strategy successor-task creation path so before persisting a new pending task it normalizes the proposed title and queue text against the parent task's recorded `failure_context.failed_step` or first failed plan step. If the candidate is a copied prefix or near-exact step-text match, reject it or require a concise board-ready rewrite before writing to `codex-memory/tasks.json`. Leave approval, queue execution, duplicate detection for already-well-formed tasks, and unrelated task creation unchanged.
success_criteria: A successor task whose proposed title or queue text matches, starts with, or is a normalized copy of the parent task's recorded failed_step is rejected or rewritten before it is written to `codex-memory/tasks.json`.
Well-formed successor tasks with concise titles still persist to `pending_approval` unchanged.
The rejection or rewrite path is deterministic for the same parent failure input and does not create duplicate board items.
A malformed step-text candidate does not increase the number of persisted board tasks when the guard triggers.
Existing approval and queue handoff behavior for already-persisted tasks remains unchanged.

tasks.title: Block duplicate strategy experiments before board persistence
tasks.reason: Historical board data still shows duplicate strategy experiments landing under new task ids before any approval or learning changed. The current snapshot remains 14 total tasks with 5 `pending_approval`, 9 `failed`, and 0 `approved`, while task-001, task-010, and task-011 share the same mobile-dashboard title, `root_source_task_id`, and `strategy_template` before each exhausted retries; task-006 and task-008 repeated the same failure-context experiment with the same `root_source_task_id` and `strategy_template` pattern. The active board now has only five approval-ready rows, so another equivalent pending task would consume 20% more review capacity without changing the underlying work. The latest persisted strategy artifact at `codex-logs/strategy-latest.json` also returned `board_tasks: []` with the message that no strategy board changes were needed, even though this lane's prompt requires every STRATEGY run to create or refresh at least one board-ready task. That evidence shows the duplicate guard still needs an explicit refresh path instead of silently no-oping when it sees an equivalent experiment.
tasks.hypothesis: If strategy derives experiment equivalence from deterministic fields on active board items (`pending_approval` or `approved`) and refreshes those rows instead of appending a new task id or returning a silent no-op, then the board can still propose improved follow-ups after failures while preventing duplicate approval items from consuming scarce review bandwidth.
tasks.experiment: Change only the strategy board-persistence path so before writing a new strategy task it compares the candidate against existing `pending_approval` and `approved` tasks using deterministic equivalence signals already on the board: normalized title, category, project, `root_source_task_id`, `strategy_template`, and a normalized experiment fingerprint when present. When an equivalent active-board task already exists, refresh that task's reasoning or history and report that existing task id in `board_tasks` instead of creating a new task record or returning no board changes. Leave failed-task follow-up creation, approval, execution, and non-equivalent task creation unchanged.
success_criteria: When a strategy candidate matches an existing `pending_approval` or `approved` board task on the chosen deterministic equivalence signals, the system refreshes that active task instead of appending a new item to `codex-memory/tasks.json`.

Equivalent candidates that share normalized title, project, category, `root_source_task_id`, and `strategy_template` with an existing active-board task resolve to the existing task id rather than creating a new one.
Failed tasks do not block a new pending follow-up when no equivalent `pending_approval` or `approved` task exists.
Non-equivalent strategy tasks still persist normally to `pending_approval`.
Repeated generations of the same experiment no longer create multiple active board items with the same normalized title, root source, and strategy template combination.
Refreshing an equivalent task appends deterministic audit history to the existing item so the latest rationale is preserved without duplicating active board rows.
When an equivalent candidate is detected, the STRATEGY output reports the refreshed existing task in `board_tasks` instead of returning an empty board-change list.
Approval, queue handoff, and execution behavior for already-persisted tasks remain unchanged.
tasks.rollback: Restore the current strategy persistence behavior so each generated candidate creates a separate board task again.

tasks.title: Cap queue retries at two attempts
tasks.reason: Historical execution data still shows retry-cap drift from mixed counters: task-006, task-007, and task-008 each ended failed with `execution.max_retries: 2`, `execution.attempt: 2`, and only two `execute_start` history entries, yet their paired `execution_context.attempts` still reached `3`. The current board snapshot remains at 14 total tasks with 5 `pending_approval`, 9 `failed`, and 0 `approved`, while `codex-learning/metrics.json` still reports success_rate=0.17 with manual_recovery_records=10, so this mismatch is still consuming scarce execution capacity and weakening retry metrics.
tasks.hypothesis: If queue retry eligibility and final failure persistence both derive from one deterministic attempt counter, tasks will stop recording a phantom third attempt after exhausting two real starts, which should reduce wasted execution time, preserve approval bandwidth for the 5 pending tasks, and make retry-related metrics trustworthy again.
tasks.experiment: Change only the queue retry-cap path so the scheduler and final failure write both use the same persisted attempt counter, refuse a third queue handoff once that counter reaches `max_retries: 2`, and keep `execution` plus `execution_context` aligned on the final attempt count. Leave task creation, approval, dashboard rendering, and unrelated failure fields unchanged.
success_criteria: A task with `max_retries` set to 2 is never queued or started for a third execution attempt.
Retry eligibility and final attempt persistence are both computed from one deterministic persisted attempt counter rather than mixed fields.
After a two-attempt failure, the persisted final attempt count matches at `2` across `execution.attempt` and `execution_context.attempts`.
A failed task that exhausts `max_retries: 2` records exactly two `execute_start` history entries before reaching its final failure state.
Two-attempt failures still preserve their final outcome and timestamps unchanged apart from aligned attempt counts.
Approval flow and first-attempt execution behavior remain unchanged for existing tasks.
tasks.rollback: Restore the previous retry-eligibility logic so the queue can schedule retries from its current sources again.

The board shows smaller actionable UI work instead of only failed umbrella tasks.
